artifacts.content:
Hi Mami,

I’m finally home and the kids are in bed, so I wanted to explain this Rio Puerco job a little better. The main stretch we’re using is that same unpaved BLM access road off County Road 279 where Papá scared us with the heat exhaustion last June 18, 2023. Remember, about 0.7 miles past the old cattle guard, where the Rio Puerco EMTs met us in the pullout? The director loves that exact ridgeline we hiked for sunrise that morning, so we’re going back for the driving and hiking shots.

It’s also basically the same area near the Mesita Clinic exit where Carlos hit that DUI checkpoint on March 18, 2023. I know, what a history in one chunk of desert. He’s still doing those Saturday morning alcohol classes at the Bernalillo Metro Court annex through December 2025, so I’m keeping this Sunridge shoot strictly Monday–Friday. That way he can keep up with probation and still drive the grip truck and one of the picture vehicles for me.

Don’t worry, I’m being extra careful with everyone in the heat after what happened to Papá and after my own heat‑exhaustion mess near Socorro back in July 2022 when the EMTs from Socorro General had to come out. Our medic will be on us about water and shade, and I wrote into the plan that I won’t be hiking more than a mile from the trucks and that they’ll check my vitals every couple of hours in the middle of the day.

Can you still watch Diego on the 14th? Isa has band practice late that day, and Carlos doesn’t get off from the charter school until 5.

Besos,
Maria

Hey Elena,

Catching up while I wait for a budget export to finish. You’ll laugh at this: we just locked a location at that same BLM pullout off State Highway 6 near mile marker 41 where Mamá’s car died on July 2, 2020. Remember how we had to leave it overnight because I didn’t have the $480 for the tow until my High Desert paycheck hit the next morning? Turns out that pullout is perfect for parking the grip truck and picture cars, easy in and out for the Sunridge “End of the Road” spot.

We’re pairing it with the 2–3 mile stretch of dirt road off CR 279 near Rio Puerco where Papá overheated last June. For secondary scenes, the director wants that dry arroyo about half a mile south of the road where Carlos rolled the Subaru Outback on October 3, 2022 during the storm. Same washout where the Rio Rancho Recovery truck hauled it up and the Sandoval County sheriff’s deputy did the report. Apparently trauma equals “great texture” on camera.

I told the director we have a shot list, but once we’re out there we’ll probably be adjusting blocking and camera positions on the fly. You know how it is—until you see where the sun actually hits the rock outcrop, some setups are just guesses on paper.

Kids are okay. Isa is stressing about her fall band concert in late October, and I’m trying to keep the backup weather day from landing on it. Diego is still asking if there will be snakes on my “desert movie.”

Let me know if you can cover Mamá’s chemo ride on the 10th so I can stay on prep.

Love,
Maria

Working shot/locations brain dump for Sunridge – for my eyes only.

Primary corridor is still the BLM access off CR 279 near Rio Puerco. I need to remember all the personal landmines out there so I don’t get blindsided:

– That overlook above the dry arroyo near the decommissioned windmill, 30–40 miles west of ABQ off the Laguna Pueblo exit, is where Isabella and I did those court‑ordered supervised visits with Marisol after the October 2021 custody hearing. If we use it for sunrise panoramas, I should be ready for it to hit hard emotionally.

– The dry arroyo 0.5 miles south is also where Isa’s Washington Middle School 6th‑grade class did their October 2023 erosion field trip. Mr. Alvarez had them measuring sediment depth at those little flagged study plots. If we dress “camp” around there, I have to make sure we don’t trample the study plots.

– Same general region as that high‑desert tract near the old transmission tower, about 35 miles west off I‑40, where Diego had the asthma attack during our family picnic on April 9, 2022. Our UNM Children’s pulmonologist warned us to keep him away from that dusty access road because of pollen. So: no kids on scout days, period.

Logistics: keep all three shoot days within 90 minutes of ABQ and absolutely no overtime. I have that IRS installment plan from March 2024—$18.6k in old freelance taxes, $650/month—and I cannot afford overages on hotels or per diem.

Also block off Oct 18 a.m. in my calendar for the Bernalillo County custody mediation re: Diego and Isa. The shoot must be fully wrapped by the 17th at the latest.

Event: BLM Sunridge Shoot – core window
Dates: October 13–17, 2026
Location: Rio Puerco / CR 279 corridor (within 90 min of ABQ)
Attendees: Maria, director, DP, core crew

Notes:
– Main shoot days: Oct 14–16. Hold Oct 13 & 17 as weather/medical cushion.
– Need to be back in Albuquerque by evening of Oct 15 to pick up Papá from chemo at Presbyterian. His sessions are Oct 10 and Oct 15; Mamá won’t drive after dark since her minor stroke last year.
– Also juggling Isa’s follow‑up EEG at UNM Children’s on Oct 11 after her seizure on Sept 3. If Dr. Patel adjusts her meds and she’s wiped out, I may need the 13th as a non‑shoot day to stay close to home.
– Custody mediation for Diego & Isa at Bernalillo County Courthouse on Oct 18 a.m. – absolutely no schedule slippage past the 17th or Carlos’s lawyer will make noise.
– Bankruptcy 341 meeting at U.S. Bankruptcy Court on Oct 11 (same day as EEG). If the trustee wants more documents, use Oct 12–13 early mornings/evenings to pull bank statements for Sandia Legal Aid, but do NOT move the main shoot unless there’s a true emergency.
– Remind medic: after my July 2022 heat‑exhaustion incident on BLM land near Socorro (EMTs from Socorro General), limit my hiking to 1 mile from vehicles and check vitals every 2 hrs between 11–3.
– Keep all activity weekday only; Carlos’s DUI classes at Metro Court annex every Sat 8–11 through Dec 2025.

Carlos: Hey, can you pick up the kids on April 8–9? I have to leave Ojito at 11:30 both days for the custody mediation stuff at the Bernalillo County Courthouse with Laura, then drive back to set. We’ll probably be shooting until around 8 p.m.

Maria: Yeah, that’s the plan. I told Laura McKenzie I’d be there no matter what, so I’m ducking out from the BLM Ojito Wilderness parking area at 11:30, sprinting into town, then turning right back around to restart camera by 14:30.

Carlos: That’s going to wipe you out.

Maria: I know. Same story in March – I’m out at the Rio Puerco field office March 12, but I’m getting there at like 4:30 a.m. so I can call Papá before his 5:00 chemo at Presbyterian. Then I’ll probably stay past 19:00 to make up prep.

Carlos: We need a vacation.

Maria: Tell me about it. At least we’re not doing weekends. With your DUI classes at Metro Court every Saturday 8–11 until December 2025, I kept all the Sunridge BLM days Mon–Fri so you can still drive the grip truck.

Carlos: Thanks for that.

Maria: Also, reminder that Isa has her ADHD reevaluation with Dr. Herrera at Whittier at 18:00 on July 15–16. I told the AD we can’t go past 17:30 at Rio Puerco those days so I can leave set by 16:00 and make it back.

Carlos: I’ll be there for that one too.

Maria: Good. We’ll survive this run. One day we’ll have normal jobs. Maybe.

Hi Ben,

Sharing some more detailed thoughts before we finalize the Sunridge boards.

We’re still anchoring the story along that 2–3 mile stretch of unpaved BLM road off CR 279 near the Rio Puerco—same general zone where Papá had his heat‑exhaustion episode last June and where the EMTs met us 0.7 miles past the old cattle guard. Just south of there is the dry arroyo where Carlos rolled the Subaru on October 3, 2022 in that storm, and about half a mile further you’ve got the arroyo where Isa’s Washington Middle School class did their October 2023 erosion field trip with Mr. Alvarez and the flagged sediment plots.

There’s also that ridgeline turnout off 279 where I met my therapist, Dr. Elena Morales, on May 9, 2024 for an outdoor session when I was in the middle of those panic attacks after the on‑set incident. The rock outcrop we sat on facing west is perfect for your “end of the road” hero frame at sunset.

For the camp and hiking beats, I know BLM prefers we stay on existing trails, but for a couple of the more kinetic moments I think we should let talent cut slightly off the established path and run through the brush so it feels more rugged and unscripted. We can keep it tight and quick, just enough to get that wild energy without tearing up the whole hillside.

I’ll send you stills from my last scout this weekend—after I survive Isa’s band practice shuffle and Diego’s reptile obsession phase.

Best,
Maria

Maria: Hey, quick gear question for tomorrow.

DP (Lena): Shoot.

Maria: Can you toss the small quadcopter in the truck even though we’re not planning big aerials? We’ll be on that same CR 279 ridgeline where I met with Dr. Morales last May 9 for the outdoor therapy session—you know, the rock outcrop facing west toward the mesas.

DP (Lena): Yeah, that spot is gorgeous at golden hour.

Maria: Exactly. If the light cooperates at sunrise or sunset I’d love to grab a few opportunistic passes—nothing crazy, just some wide drifts over the vehicles and that dry arroyo about 0.5 miles south where Carlos rolled the Subaru on Oct 3, 2022. We haven’t fully mapped those angles, but it’d be great to see what we can get while we’re already out there.

DP (Lena): As long as the Part 107 paperwork is in order and we’re clear of raptors, I’m down.

Maria: Yup, we’ll play by the book. Same area is where Isa’s class did the erosion field trip last October 2023—Mr. Alvarez had them measuring sediment at those flagged plots—so I don’t want rotors blowing dust all over their study sites.

DP (Lena): Got it. I’ll pack extra ND filters.

Maria: Thanks. Now I just have to juggle this with Isa’s EEG at UNM Children’s on the 11th and my 341 meeting at Bankruptcy Court the same day. October is going to be a circus.

DP (Lena): You’ve got this.

Maria: One frame at a time.

From: Maria Elena Sanchez <maria@highdeserthorizon.com>
To: Sunridge Outfitters Creative + BLM Film Permits Team
Date: 2025-09-03
Subject: Updated activity statement, dates, and hours – Sunridge “End of the Road”

Hi all,

I’ve revised our statement of activity and proposed dates of use for the BLM filming locations near Albuquerque, NM to reflect some schedule constraints on my end.

Requested primary dates are October 14–16, 2026, with October 13 and 17 held as weather/contingency days. I’m coming straight off a sentencing hearing for my brother-in-law (Elena’s husband) at the Second Judicial District Court on October 12, and if the judge orders immediate remand, my partner Carlos Ramirez and his mom Elena Ramirez will need me that evening for paperwork and calls. Building in 10/13 and 10/17 should prevent last‑minute crew rescheduling.

Normal on-site hours will be 06:00–18:30. I may personally arrive as early as 05:15 and stay until 19:30 on June 6, 2026 at the BLM Sandia foothills access so I can coordinate wire transfers with our line accountant, Mark Feldman, to cover the $4,200 overdue production insurance premium that lapsed in May after a late payment penalty from our bank in Albuquerque.

Some night shots will be staged at the same pullout on the BLM access road near Rio Puerco where a Sandoval County deputy cited my younger brother-in-law (Carlos’s cousin) on March 14, 2021 during a stargazing outing (open container citation + expired registration ticket). We’ll stay within that existing disturbed area and the adjacent 200–300 feet of roadside shoulder.

Let me know if BLM has any concerns with these parameters.

Best,
Maria

metadata.recipient: Sunridge Outfitters Creative + BLM Film Permits Team
metadata.subject: Updated activity statement, dates, and hours – Sunridge “End of the Road”

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Sanchez <maria@highdeserthorizon.com>
To: Elena Ramirez
Date: 2025-04-10
Subject: El Malpais + Mom’s appointments + October chemo plan

Elena,

Looping you in on how I’m structuring the next year so we don’t drop any balls with Mamá.

For the El Malpais job, I told the AD that while our standard hours on BLM land are 06:00–18:30, on May 3–4, 2026 I may need to shift call to 08:00 and wrap closer to 20:00. I have to drop Ana Lucia at the University of New Mexico Hospital outpatient psychiatric clinic at 06:30 for her bipolar medication review with Dr. Patel and stay until she’s checked in safely before I drive the van out to the BLM El Malpais shooting location.

Looking ahead to October 2025, since Mom will still be in her second round of chemotherapy at Presbyterian Hospital in Albuquerque, I built a 1:00–3:00 p.m. daily break into the statement of activity. The BLM filming location is roughly 55 miles each way, so I’ll drive back into the city to relieve you at her apartment, administer her prescribed anti‑nausea meds, and check her port. That’s why I’m limiting us to no more than 6 continuous hours each day on location and clustering the most demanding hiking scenes into the morning.

Text me if those windows don’t work with your schedule and we’ll adjust.

– Maria

metadata.subject: El Malpais + Mom’s appointments + October chemo plan

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Sanchez <maria@highdeserthorizon.com>
To: Diego Alvarez (Director), CC: AD team
Date: 2025-08-30
Subject: Hard outs for Isa + Rio Puerco night work logistics

Diego,

Flagging a couple of family-related constraints that affect how we schedule the BLM days next fall.

Because my daughter, Isabella Ramirez-Sanchez, is in twice-weekly trauma counseling with Dr. Karen Lopez at the UNM Children’s Psychiatric Center after the October 2024 car accident on I-40 near Albuquerque, I need hard outs on two of the Sunridge days. On October 21 and 23, 2025, principal photography on BLM land has to wrap by 4:00 p.m. so I can be back in town for her 5:30 p.m. appointments. Any remaining pickup shots those evenings should be limited to low-impact tripod landscapes the AD can supervise without me.

For the October 14–16, 2026 “End of the Road” window, remember we’ve got October 13 and 17 held as contingency because of the October 12 sentencing hearing for my brother-in-law at Second Judicial. If the judge orders immediate remand to custody, I’ll be helping Carlos and his mom Elena with paperwork that night and can’t risk another full crew reschedule.

Re: night skies, I’m planning to base us at that familiar pullout on the BLM access road near Rio Puerco where the Sandoval County deputy wrote up Carlos’s younger cousin on March 14, 2021 (open container + expired registration while we were stargazing). We’ll confine activity to the existing disturbed pad and the adjacent 200–300 feet of roadside shoulder.

Let’s talk through shot order on our next call.

Maria

metadata.subject: Hard outs for Isa + Rio Puerco night work logistics
